name: Mokosh
description: Mokosh, also known as Mokusha, is a major deity in Slavic mythology, representing the Earth Mother and associated with fertility, women's work, weaving, spinning, fate, and protection. She is considered one of the most important goddesses in the Slavic pantheon, often depicted as a powerful and nurturing figure. Her origins are ancient, possibly predating the common Slavic period. Mokosh is believed to control the moisture of the earth, essential for agriculture and the prosperity of the community. She is a patron of women and childbirth, ensuring healthy pregnancies and safe deliveries. As a goddess of fate, she is linked to the spinning of destiny's thread, influencing the course of lives. Mokosh is often associated with the moist earth and dark places, connecting her to the underworld and the spirits of ancestors. She is revered for her ability to provide sustenance and protection, ensuring the continuation of life and the well-being of her followers. While not always explicitly mentioned in surviving texts, her importance is inferred from archaeological findings, folklore, and comparative mythology. Mokosh is a complex figure, embodying both the life-giving and potentially destructive aspects of nature. Offerings to Mokosh often involved flax, wool, and other materials related to women's crafts.
subtitle: Earth Mother, Weaver of Fate

keyMyths.title: Mokosh and the Harvest
keyMyths.description: Mokosh ensures a bountiful harvest by blessing the fields with rain and fertile soil. Farmers pray to her for a successful growing season.

keyMyths.title: Mokosh and the Spinning Wheel
keyMyths.description: Mokosh is the patron of spinners and weavers, guiding their hands to create beautiful and strong fabrics. She also spins the threads of fate, influencing the lives of mortals.

keyMyths.title: Mokosh and Childbirth
keyMyths.description: Women invoke Mokosh during childbirth for protection and a safe delivery. She is believed to ease the pain and ensure the health of both mother and child.

sources.author: Marija Gimbutas
